questions.text: When faced with a complex problem, what is your first instinct?

options.text: Break it down into smaller, logical parts.
Brainstorm unique and out-of-the-box solutions.
Look for a hands-on, immediate fix.
Gather the team to discuss and delegate tasks.

questions.text: Which of these activities sounds most appealing to you?

options.text: Writing a script to automate a tedious task.
Designing the layout and colors of a new website.
Analyzing data to find trends and patterns.
Presenting a project pitch to stakeholders.

questions.text: How do you prefer to learn something new?

options.text: Reading detailed documentation and tutorials.
Experimenting visually until it looks right.
Understanding the underlying theory and logic.
Discussing it with peers or mentors.

questions.text: In a group project, what role do you usually take?

options.text: The architect: planning the structure and systems.
The visionary: coming up with the big ideas.
The coordinator: keeping everyone on track.
The doer: putting your head down and getting work done.

questions.text: What type of puzzles do you enjoy most?

options.text: Logic grids or Sudoku.
Jigsaw puzzles or visual spatial games.
Strategy board games with others.
Fixing something broken in the real world.

questions.text: How do you handle failure or a roadblock?

options.text: Debug the issue systematically to find the root cause.
Pivot completely and try a radically different approach.
Review the data and metrics to see what went wrong.
Ask for feedback from others and adjust the plan.

questions.text: What environment do you thrive in?

options.text: A quiet space with multiple monitors and deep focus.
A vibrant, visually inspiring studio.
A dynamic setting with constant communication.
At the front of a room, leading a workshop.

questions.text: When looking at a new app, what do you notice first?

options.text: How fast it loads and its underlying architecture.
How intuitive and beautiful the interface is.
How it organizes and presents its data.
How it connects users together.

questions.text: Which tool sounds most exciting to master?

options.text: A programming language or cloud platform.
A design suite like Figma or Photoshop.
A data visualization tool like Tableau or Pandas.
A project management tool like Jira or Trello.

questions.text: What is your ultimate career goal?

options.text: To build robust, scalable, and secure systems.
To create experiences that delight and inspire users.
To discover hidden truths that drive major decisions.
To lead a company or team to success.

questions.text: How do you prefer to manage a complex project?

options.text: By automating the tracking and progress updates.
By visualizing the roadmap and keeping it flexible.
By breaking it down into measurable milestones and KPIs.
By holding regular sync-ups to ensure team alignment.

questions.text: When someone explains a new concept to you, what helps you understand it best?

options.text: Seeing the code, equations, or exact mechanism.
An analogy, sketch, or visual representation.
A step-by-step logical breakdown of how it works.
Discussing its real-world impact and applications.

questions.text: What is your reaction to an unexpected change in requirements?

options.text: Immediately start refactoring the core logic to adapt.
See it as an opportunity to redesign for a better user experience.
Analyze the impact on the timeline and resources.
Gather the team to negotiate and manage stakeholder expectations.

questions.text: Which of these extra-curricular activities sounds the most fun?

options.text: Participating in a hackathon or coding challenge.
Attending an art exhibit or design workshop.
Solving intricate logic puzzles or playing chess.
Organizing a community event or volunteering.

questions.text: How do you feel about working with hardware or physical components?

options.text: I love integrating software with hardware interfaces.
I enjoy designing the physical look and feel of the product.
I prefer working purely with abstract data and models.
I focus more on the team building it than the hardware itself.
